clean_story: Invisible light.  Sees through skin. "X-ray" comes from the letter X—a placeholder for the unknown, like a mystery variable in math. These rays are light so energetic they pass through soft tissue but bounce off bones and metal, like a flashlight beam cutting through fog but stopping at a brick wall. Most think of broken-arm scans, but X-rays started as a parlor trick. In 1895, Wilhelm Röntgen noticed a glowing screen across his lab while testing cathode rays——his wife’s hand bones, projected onto photographic film, became the first "X-ray image. "Here’s how it works:Electrons fired at a metal target (often tungsten) slam into atoms, knocking loose photons 10,000x more powerful than visible light. These photons zip through air, flesh, even thin wood——but dense materials absorb them, casting shadows on detectors. Airport scanners use backscatter X-rays to reveal hidden knives or explosives by catching rays that "bounce back" like echoes. Dentists aim low-dose beams at teeth, where fillings glow white against darker enamel. Too much exposure damages DNA——yet controlled doses let doctors spot tumors smaller than a grain of rice. Even art historians use them to uncover hidden sketches beneath famous paintings. Three related subjects are radiation risks, medical imaging and electron beams.
